content.text: By default, a CrewAI run is stateless — every
starts from scratch with no knowledge of previous runs. Enable
on the Crew to activate four complementary memory stores that let agents learn, recall entities, and build context across tasks and sessions.
to the Crew constructor. Agents automatically write to and read from the memory stores without any extra code.

from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

researcher = Agent(
    role="Research Specialist",
    goal="Gather information on {topic}",
    backstory="An expert who builds on prior research sessions.",
    memory=True,  # individual agent memory flag (also needed)
    verbose=True,
)

task = Task(
    description="Research the latest developments in {topic}",
    expected_output="A structured summary with key findings",
    agent=researcher,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher],
    tasks=[task],
    memory=True,        # activates all memory stores for this crew
    verbose=True,
)

result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "quantum computing"})
RAG retrieval over recent context within this run
Recall facts and decisions from previous runs
Auto-extracts named entities (people, orgs, facts) and tracks them
Composite view combining short-term + long-term + entity for full context
Persistent per-user preferences and history (optional, requires Mem0)
Stored as vector embeddings, short-term memory captures what happened in the
and makes it retrievable by semantic similarity. Agents query it automatically when formulating each response.

# Short-term memory is enabled by default when memory=True
# Nothing extra to configure — CrewAI handles embedding and retrieval
crew = Crew(agents=[researcher, analyst], tasks=[...], memory=True)

# Internally, CrewAI embeds each task result and stores it in a
# ChromaDB collection. When an agent starts a new task, it queries
# the collection with its current task description and injects the
# top-K results into the prompt.
using SQLite. Agents recall what they learned in previous sessions, avoiding redundant research and maintaining consistency.

# Run 1 — researcher learns something new
result1 = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "fusion energy"})

# Run 2 (later, same crew) — researcher recalls prior findings
# and builds on them instead of starting from scratch
result2 = crew.kickoff(inputs={"topic": "fusion energy investment trends"})
# The agent's prompt includes retrieved long-term memories about fusion energy

Entity memory automatically extracts named entities — people, organisations, products, locations — and stores structured facts about them. When those entities appear again, the agent is briefed on what was already learned.

# Entity memory extraction happens automatically in the background.
# If a task output mentions "Dr. Sarah Chen, lead researcher at MIT",
# the entity memory stores:
#   entity: "Dr. Sarah Chen"
#   type: Person
#   facts: ["lead researcher at MIT"]
#
# In a later task mentioning "Dr. Chen", the agent receives:
#   "Memory: Dr. Sarah Chen is the lead researcher at MIT (from prior run)"
Memory stores require an embedding model to convert text into vectors. The default is OpenAI's
User memory tracks per-user preferences and history across multiple crew sessions. It requires the Mem0 platform (cloud or self-hosted) and an API key.

import os
os.environ["MEM0_API_KEY"] = "m0-..."

from crewai import Crew, Agent, Task

# Provide a user_id to namespace memories per user
crew = Crew(
    agents=[assistant],
    tasks=[chat_task],
    memory=True,
    # Crew automatically uses Mem0 for user memory when MEM0_API_KEY is set
)

# Pass user_id at runtime
result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"query": "plan my trip", "user_id": "user-42"})
or call the method from Python to clear stores between experiments.

# Clear all memory for a crew
crewai reset-memories --all

# Clear only long-term memory
crewai reset-memories --long-term

# Clear only short-term memory
crewai reset-memories --short-term

# Clear entity memory
crewai reset-memories --entities
By default, long-term and entity memory databases are stored in:
. You can override the storage path:

import os
# Override before creating the crew
os.environ["CREWAI_STORAGE_DIR"] = "/app/data/crewai"

crew = Crew(agents=[...], tasks=[...], memory=True)
# databases land in /app/data/crewai/<crew_name>/
Memory adds latency because each task requires an embedding call and a vector search. Measure the trade-off:
: retrieved memories add tokens to each prompt — monitor token usage
For single-run pipelines with no cross-session need, skip
For long-running research assistants or customer support bots that accumulate knowledge, the latency is worthwhile.

# First run builds knowledge; subsequent runs recall and extend it
for session in ["initial research", "follow-up", "final analysis"]:
    result = crew.kickoff(inputs={"phase": session, "topic": "AI regulation"})
    print(f"[{session}] {result.raw[:200]}")
    # long-term memory accumulates findings across sessions

# Task 1 mentions "Anthropic (AI safety company, founded 2021)"
# Task 2 later: "What does Anthropic say about..." —
# agent automatically recalls entity facts about Anthropic

researcher = Agent(role="Analyst", goal="...", backstory="...", memory=True)
task1 = Task(description="Summarise the key AI safety labs and their positions",
             expected_output="...", agent=researcher)
task2 = Task(description="What does Anthropic say about AGI timelines?",
             expected_output="...", agent=researcher)

crew = Crew(agents=[researcher], tasks=[task1, task2], memory=True)
result = crew.kickoff()  # entity "Anthropic" is known when task2 runs
Understanding the retrieval mechanism helps you tune prompts and embedder choices for better recall.
When an agent starts a task, CrewAI:
Embeds the current task description using the configured embedder.
Queries the short-term ChromaDB collection with the embedding, fetching the top-K most similar past results from this run.
Queries the long-term SQLite/vector store for semantically similar results from previous runs.
Runs a keyword extraction pass over the task description and looks up matching entity records.
Concatenates all retrieved snippets into a 'Memory' section prepended to the agent's system prompt.

The retrieval is similarity-based, not exhaustive. If a past result is not semantically close to the current task, it won't be retrieved — even if it is technically relevant. Write task descriptions with specific, concrete terminology to maximise recall precision.
CrewAI's memory stores are pluggable. You can swap the default ChromaDB/SQLite backends for production-grade alternatives.

from crewai.memory import LongTermMemory
from crewai.memory.storage.rag_storage import RAGStorage

# Custom RAG storage backed by Pinecone instead of the default ChromaDB
custom_storage = RAGStorage(
    type="long-term",
    allow_reset=True,
    embedder_config={
        "provider": "openai",
        "config": {"model": "text-embedding-3-small"},
    },
    # path specifies where ChromaDB stores its files on disk
    path="/app/data/pinecone_mirror",
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher],
    tasks=[task],
    memory=True,
    long_term_memory=LongTermMemory(storage=custom_storage),
)
During development, read the raw SQLite database to understand what the memory stores have accumulated.

import sqlite3, os

db_path = os.path.expanduser("~/.crewai/ResearchCrew/long_term_memory_storage.db")

conn = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
cursor = conn.cursor()

# List all stored memories
cursor.execute("SELECT agent, task, metadata, datetime FROM long_term_memories ORDER BY datetime DESC LIMIT 20")
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    agent, task, meta, dt = row
    print(f"[{dt}] {agent}: {task[:80]}")

conn.close()
store is ChromaDB — inspect it via the ChromaDB Python client:

import chromadb

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path=os.path.expanduser("~/.crewai/ResearchCrew"))
for coll in client.list_collections():
    print(coll.name, "—", coll.count(), "documents")

In a multi-agent crew, each agent has its own long-term and entity memory namespace. The short-term and contextual stores are shared across the crew so all agents can access results from other agents' tasks in the current run.

researcher = Agent(role="Researcher", goal="...", backstory="...", memory=True)
analyst   = Agent(role="Analyst",    goal="...", backstory="...", memory=True)
writer    = Agent(role="Writer",     goal="...", backstory="...", memory=True)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, analyst, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, analysis_task, writing_task],
    memory=True,
)

# When analyst runs analysis_task:
# - Short-term memory contains researcher's result from research_task
# - Long-term memory contains analyst's own prior sessions
# - Entity memory contains entities extracted from both agents' outputs
serve different roles — it is not an either-or choice:
Guaranteed task-to-task data flow in current run
Top-K similar past results (may miss some)
Full output of every listed task (never misses)
No extra latency (text already in memory)
to guarantee critical data flows between tasks in the
and to surface related context automatically.
When memory retrieval produces wrong or irrelevant context, diagnose with these steps:
— the logs show which memories were retrieved and injected into each prompt.
— if your task descriptions use vague language, similarity scores drop. Make descriptions specific.
— stale memories from prior experiments can mislead agents. Run
— count documents and peek at stored embeddings to verify what is actually stored.
— by default CrewAI retrieves multiple results. If irrelevant items are injected, try a smaller K by subclassing
Retrieved memories are prepended to the agent's system prompt. Each memory snippet adds tokens. On long-running crews with hundreds of stored memories, you may approach context window limits.
Strategies to manage token budget with memory enabled:
) still produce accurate retrieval for domain text at a fraction of the cost of
: if using a 4k-context model, disable memory or reduce retrieved snippet count.
: consider adding a summarisation task after each long task, and use that summary as the agent's reported output — what gets stored in memory is the shorter summary, not the full 2000-word response.
between batches to keep the short-term store lean.
on both the agent and the Crew to activate all memory stores.
to use OpenAI, Ollama, VertexAI, or HuggingFace embeddings.
User memory (Mem0) adds per-user profiles for multi-user applications.
Measure memory latency — embedding calls add 50–200 ms per task; skip
for single-run pipelines where persistence adds no value.
